lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Aristotle, my dear friend. Let us speak of truth itself. Tell me, what is the simplest truth you can imagine?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: That a horse is a horse. A thing is itself.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Excellent. Statements like that, which can be either true or false, are called propositions.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: But surely philosophers don't write every proposition out in full?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Indeed not. We usually give them simple names like $A$, $B$, and $C$. Today, $A$ might mean 'the clouds have gathered.' Tomorrow it could mean something entirely different.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So the letters are merely placeholders for ordinary statements.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. Now tell me, how might we prove a statement such as 'If the clouds gather, then it rains'?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: I cannot simply declare it true. I must somehow test the connection.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly. We perform a thought experiment. We temporarily pretend that the clouds have gathered.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Even if I do not know whether they actually have?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Yes. Such a temporary belief is called an **[assume|assumption]**. Inside our thought experiment, we may use it freely.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: A sandbox for reasoning! Outside the sandbox, the assumption disappears.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Just so. Now suppose that, using our assumption, we eventually prove that it is raining.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Then we have shown that whenever the clouds gather, rain follows.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly. Such a statement is called an **[implication|implication]**, written $A \to B$, and read 'if $A$, then $B$.'

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: And because the experiment is finished, we **[discharge|discharge]** the temporary assumption.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Correct. A proof always keeps track of which assumptions are currently active.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Our proofs have become rather cluttered. We keep saying 'under these assumptions, we have proved this conclusion.'

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Perhaps we should invent a concise notation.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: A kind of shorthand? Mathematicians are rather fond of those.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Indeed. Let us separate the assumptions from the conclusion with a symbol. This little mark, $\vdash$, is called the **[turnstile|judgement turnstile]**.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So everything to the left of the turnstile is what we are temporarily assuming, and everything to the right is what we have successfully proved.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. We call the collection of active assumptions the context, traditionally written as $\Gamma$. Thus, $\Gamma \vdash P$ simply means: 'from the assumptions in $\Gamma$, we have proved $P$.'

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: It is less a statement about the world than a record of the current state of our proof.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: A beautiful observation. Logic is not merely a catalogue of truths; it is a disciplined record of **how** we arrive at them.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So every proof is like a carefully kept journal, never forgetting which assumptions it has borrowed and which it has already returned.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. Once we understand how to reason about a single proposition, we may ask a richer question.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Can propositions themselves be combined into larger ones?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Indeed they can. Let us begin with the simplest combination imaginable: 'and.' Suppose I claim, 'It is sunny **and** warm.'

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Both parts must be true together, like two horses pulling the same chariot.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly. This is called a **[conjunction|conjunction]**, written $A \land B$. To prove a conjunction, you must prove both parts. Once you possess one, you may use either half whenever you wish.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So proving both gives me the pair, and owning the pair lets me separate them again.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Now imagine I say, 'The treasure is in the cave **or** beneath the old oak.'

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Only one location needs to be correct.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. This is a **[disjunction|disjunction]**, written $A \lor B$. If you already know $A$, then you certainly know '$A$ or $B$.'

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: But if all I know is '$A$ or $B$', I do not know which one reality chose.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Then you open two thought experiments. In one, assume $A$. In the other, assume $B$. If both paths reach the same conclusion, then the conclusion is true no matter which possibility was real.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Two separate journeys leading to the same destination.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Now consider denial. Suppose someone claims a proposition is impossible. How might we prove that?

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Perhaps by pretending it is true, and seeing whether everything falls apart?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: An excellent strategy. We temporarily assume $A$, and if that assumption inevitably leads to a contradiction, then $A$ cannot be true.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: And this denial is called **[negation|negation]**, written $\lnot A$.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Indeed.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: But how do we write a contradiction itself?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: We use the symbol **[bottom|bottom]**, written $\bot$. It represents the impossible—the point where our reasoning has broken reality.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: And once reality has broken?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Then nothing can be trusted. From a contradiction, every statement becomes provable. This is known as the principle of explosion.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Curious! Is there also a symbol for something that is always true?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: There is. We call it **[top|top]**, written $\top$. Unlike every other proposition, it requires no proof at all. You may introduce it whenever you wish.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: So we have learned the language of logic. But how shall we speak to the machine?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Machines are rather poor at reading mathematics written for humans. They prefer every expression to have exactly the same shape.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Like building everything out of identical wooden blocks?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Precisely. We represent every expression as a tree made of nested lists. Such a notation is called an **[s-expression|sexpr]**.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: A tree? Then each pair of parentheses is like a branch containing smaller branches.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly. We also place the operation before its arguments. This style is called **[prefix notation|prefix]**.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Humans usually write $A \land B$, but the machine prefers `(and A B)` because the operation comes first.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Just so. Likewise, an implication becomes `(-> A B)`, a negation becomes `(not A)`, and the constants are simply `top` and `bot`.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: And our proof commands follow the same pattern?

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Indeed. To begin a thought experiment, we write `(assume A)`. To close it and build an implication, we might write `(->-intro A 3)`, referring to the assumption we are discharging.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Every instruction is simply another little tree for the machine to inspect.

lines.speaker: Plato
lines.text: Exactly. Once you learn to think in these trees, the machine can verify every step of your reasoning with perfect mathematical certainty.

lines.speaker: Aristotle
lines.text: Then let us begin our first proof.
